title: Confidence-Guided Stepwise Model Routing for Cost-Efficient Reasoning (STEER)

background.problem_context: Existing stepwise model routing methods (RSD, SpecReason) depend on either trained Process Reward Models (PRMs) or LLM-as-judge evaluation, both of which introduce external model dependencies. PRMs suffer from domain shift when applied to tasks outside their training distribution, and LLM-as-judge adds significant latency per step. A routing signal that is model-internal, training-free, and domain-agnostic would be ideal.
prior_limitations: PRM-based methods (RSD, TRIM) depend on external trained models that suffer from domain shift and require costly training data collection (e.g., Monte Carlo rollouts)
LLM-as-judge methods (SpecReason) require a full LLM forward pass for verification, adding per-step latency
Generate-then-verify paradigm incurs sunk costs: at low acceptance rates, total FLOPs can exceed using the large model alone (since both small and large model are invoked)
No prior method uses model-internal signals for routing without any external model or training
background.key_observation: The small model's own logit distribution contains rich information about its confidence. When the model is confident (high max logit), it is likely generating a correct step; when uncertain (low max logit), the step is likely erroneous and should be routed to a larger model. This confidence signal is free to compute (available from the generation forward pass) and domain-agnostic.

motivation: Existing step-level routing methods depend on external trained models (PRM) or expensive LLM-as-judge verification, limiting their domain robustness and introducing additional compute overhead. STEER proposes that the small model's own logit-based confidence is a superior routing signal: it is free to compute (extracted directly from the generation forward pass), requires no external model, is inherently domain-agnostic, and correlates well with step correctness. By calibrating confidence distributions with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), STEER achieves the routing quality of PRM/LLM-judge methods without their costs and domain limitations.
novelty_claims: First domain-agnostic, external model-free stepwise routing framework that uses only model-internal confidence signals
Introduction of GMM-based posterior routing: confidence distributions are modeled as a mixture of 'confident' and 'unconfident' Gaussians, enabling probabilistic routing decisions
Probe-then-dispatch paradigm: routing decision is made BEFORE full step generation, avoiding the sunk cost of generate-then-verify methods
Demonstrates effectiveness across both math reasoning AND general reasoning tasks, unlike PRM-based methods that are math-specific
problem_statement: Given a small model M_w and a large model M_s, route individual reasoning steps to the appropriate model based solely on model-internal confidence signals, without requiring any external routing model, PRM, or training data collection.
core_idea: Extract logit-based confidence scores from the small model during generation. Compute token-level confidence as φ_ij = max(z_ij) (the maximum logit value for each token). Aggregate to step-level confidence Φ_i by averaging over relevant tokens (math expression tokens for math tasks, all tokens for general tasks). Model the distribution of step-level confidence scores as a two-component Gaussian Mixture Model: one Gaussian for 'confident' steps and one for 'unconfident' steps. Compute the posterior probability P(confident|Φ_i) and route to the large model when this falls below a threshold γ.
extended_summary: STEER identifies that the small model's maximum logit values contain rich confidence information that is inherently available during generation at zero additional cost. For each token j in step i, the token-level confidence φ_ij is the maximum value in the logit vector (before softmax). The step-level confidence Φ_i aggregates these: for math domains, only logits of mathematical expression tokens are averaged (to avoid dilution by formatting tokens); for general domains, all tokens are averaged. The distribution of step-level confidences across all steps in a batch naturally forms a bimodal pattern — confident steps cluster high, unconfident steps cluster low. STEER fits a two-component GMM to this distribution and computes the posterior P(confident|Φ_i). Steps where P(confident|Φ_i) < γ are routed to the large model. The GMM fitting is done on-the-fly for each batch (group of problems), making it adaptive to the data distribution without any offline training. Key advantage: routing latency is O(1) (a single GMM probability computation), versus O(N) for PRM (a full model forward pass) or O(N²) for LLM-as-judge. STEER achieves +20% accuracy with 48% fewer FLOPs on AIME compared to standalone large model, and generalizes to non-math benchmarks (ACPBench, MuSiQue, KOR-Bench).

method_pipeline.name: Step generation with logit extraction
method_pipeline.description: Small model generates reasoning step. During generation, extract max logit values for each token: φ_ij = max(z_ij)

method_pipeline.name: Step-level confidence aggregation
method_pipeline.description: Aggregate token-level confidence to step-level: Φ_i = mean(φ_ij) for relevant tokens. For math: only math expression tokens. For general: all tokens

method_pipeline.name: GMM-based confidence calibration
method_pipeline.description: Fit a 2-component GMM to the distribution of step-level confidences across the current batch. This separates confident (high) and unconfident (low) steps

method_pipeline.name: Posterior routing decision
method_pipeline.description: Compute P(confident|Φ_i) for each step. If P < γ (threshold), route that step to the large model; otherwise accept the small model's step

method_pipeline.name: Large model regeneration (conditional)
method_pipeline.description: For routed steps, the large model regenerates the step from the current reasoning context. The large model's step replaces the small model's step

architecture.routing_mechanism: GMM posterior probability on logit-based confidence
key_modules: Logit extractor (from generation forward pass, zero additional cost)
Domain-aware token selector (math expressions vs all tokens)

design_choices.choice: Max logit rather than entropy or max probability as confidence metric
design_choices.rationale: Empirically outperforms both alternatives, especially in borderline scenarios. Max logit is scale-aware (preserves information about magnitude of model certainty) while probability saturates near 1.0 for confident predictions

design_choices.choice: GMM calibration rather than raw threshold on confidence
design_choices.rationale: Raw confidence scores vary significantly across model families and tasks. GMM normalizes the distribution relative to the current batch, making the threshold γ interpretable as a posterior probability regardless of absolute confidence scale

design_choices.choice: Domain-aware token selection for confidence aggregation
design_choices.rationale: Mathematical expression tokens carry the core reasoning signal. Including formatting and connector tokens ('Therefore', 'Next') dilutes the confidence signal. For general tasks, no such distinction is needed
Avoids the sunk cost problem of generate-then-verify: if routing decision is made before or during generation, compute is only spent on the model that will actually be used

key_hyperparameters: K (batch/group size for GMM fitting, robust K=1 to K=10)

quantitative_results.notes: Simultaneously +20% accuracy and -48% FLOPs vs standalone large model
STEER dominates all baselines (RSD, SpecReason, query-level router) on accuracy-FLOPs Pareto curves
STEER generalizes to non-math tasks, unlike PRM-based methods
GMM posterior computation is negligible compared to PRM forward pass (O(N)) or LLM-as-judge

baselines_compared.delta: STEER achieves better Pareto front on all benchmarks. RSD's post-hoc evaluation causes FLOPs to exceed large-model-only at low acceptance rates
STEER has orders-of-magnitude lower routing latency (O(1) vs LLM forward pass) with comparable accuracy-cost tradeoffs
Step-level routing significantly outperforms query-level routing on all metrics

ablation_findings.ablation: Confidence metric: max logit vs entropy vs max probability
ablation_findings.effect: Max logit consistently outperforms alternatives across all model families
ablation_findings.key_insight: Max logit preserves magnitude information that entropy and probability compress

ablation_findings.ablation: GMM vs raw threshold on confidence
ablation_findings.effect: GMM significantly outperforms raw thresholding, especially across model families with different confidence scales
ablation_findings.key_insight: GMM calibration is essential for cross-model generalizability

ablation_findings.ablation: Group size K for GMM fitting
ablation_findings.effect: Robust across K=1 to K=10. Even K=1 (single problem) gives reasonable results
ablation_findings.key_insight: The bimodal structure of confidence distributions is robust even with small samples

ablation_findings.effect: Math expression token selection outperforms all-token averaging for math tasks
ablation_findings.key_insight: Formatting and connector tokens are always high-confidence and dilute the signal

ablation_findings.ablation: Large model usage vs problem difficulty
ablation_findings.effect: On OmniMath, large model usage rate increases monotonically with problem difficulty (level 1→10)
ablation_findings.key_insight: STEER naturally allocates more compute to harder problems without explicit difficulty awareness

main_findings: Model-internal logit confidence is a surprisingly strong routing signal — competitive with PRM and LLM-as-judge while being free to compute and domain-agnostic
GMM calibration is essential for making confidence-based routing work across different model families and tasks
STEER achieves +20% accuracy with 48% fewer FLOPs on AIME, dominating all baselines on Pareto curves
The probe-then-dispatch paradigm avoids the sunk cost problem of generate-then-verify methods
Cross-domain generalization: STEER works on math AND general reasoning tasks, unlike PRM-based methods
limitations: Logit-based confidence is model-specific — different model families have different confidence calibration, hence the need for GMM
Step boundaries still defined by heuristic delimiters (\n\n)
Batch-level GMM fitting means routing quality may degrade for very small batches (single problem)
Max logit confidence may not capture all aspects of step quality (e.g., a model can be confidently wrong)

key_connections: STEER's zero-cost routing signal concept aligns with our zero-cost rubric scoring — both avoid external model overhead
STEER evaluates model confidence (how sure the model is), while our rubrics evaluate routing quality (whether the routing decision was good) — these are orthogonal signals
STEER's probe-then-dispatch paradigm could be combined with our rubric reward: use confidence for initial routing, rubrics for RL training
STEER's domain-agnostic advantage is relevant: our rubrics are also domain-agnostic since they evaluate routing patterns rather than reasoning content
relevance_to_our_work.advantage_of_steer: No external model dependency, lowest routing latency, works across domains
relevance_to_our_work.advantage_of_our_approach: Dense reward for RL training (rubrics provide multi-dimensional feedback), explicit routing quality evaluation rather than indirect confidence proxy
